blocks.text: Most comebacks fail for the same reason — not because the training was too hard, but because nobody drew a clear line between what was safe to push and what wasn't.

A scan doesn't draw that line. Neither does a calendar, or a date your physio circled three months ago. What actually gets an athlete back — reliably, and for good — is a small set of rules that hold up regardless of the sport or the joint involved.
Here they are.
Pain is information, not a stop sign
Training around an injury without any pain at all isn't realistic, and chasing that is how athletes end up under-loaded for months. The skill isn't avoiding pain — it's reading it correctly.

blocks.label: Rule
blocks.text: Discomfort that stays mild during the set and settles by the next morning is a green light — keep training that pattern. Anything sharp, anything that gets worse set to set, or anything still there the next day is a red flag: back off the range or load, and get it checked before you push further. That's the whole framework. Apply it every session and you stop guessing.
Not everything a scan finds is damage

Two athletes can get identical-sounding news from an MRI and need completely different plans. A torn structure — a ligament, a tendon, a meniscus — genuinely follows a healing timeline. It needs progressive load reintroduced in stages, not permanent avoidance.

A structural trait — a shape a joint has always had, a laxity that shows up on imaging — is different. It isn't injured tissue on a healing clock. It's architecture. It never resolves, because there's nothing to resolve. The job there isn't rehab, it's building permanent strength and control around it, for as long as that athlete trains.

blocks.label: Key
blocks.text: Ask which one you're dealing with before you plan a single session. Program a healing tear like permanent architecture and you leave capacity on the table. Program permanent architecture like a healing tear and you stop training the thing that actually needed it — forever, not for six weeks.
Your sport counts as training load

If an athlete is already back playing while still rehabbing in the gym, the two loads are one load — not two separate plans that happen to share a body. A program that treats the gym in isolation, as if match day doesn't exist, will eventually double-load the exact tissue it's supposed to be protecting.

items: Never schedule the heaviest gym session the day before a match or competitive session
Give the same joint or pattern real recovery between two hard exposures — gym or sport, it doesn't matter which
Treat a hard, long, or competitive match as a low-readiness signal for the next session, not a free pass to ignore it

blocks.text: Prove it solo before you pair it

Supersetting two exercises to save time is good practice — once you know what both of them can actually do. Pairing two unproven movements together to find working weight on both at once just adds friction exactly when you need clean, simple data.

blocks.label: Rule
blocks.text: Any lift that's new to an athlete — or new again, after time off — gets run as a straight, standalone set first. Find the real working weight, log it, and only start pairing it into supersets or complexes once it has a full cycle of clean data behind it.
Let the weak side lead

On any single-limb exercise during a return, the compromised side goes first — every time — and its honest output sets the dose for both sides. If the weak side manages eight clean reps, the strong side does eight too, even if it clearly has more in the tank.
Let the strong side run ahead instead, and the gap between the two sides doesn't close. It widens — quietly, for months — until it shows up as the next injury.
The comeback is staged, not switched on
There's no single moment where an athlete goes from hurt to fine. A real return arc moves through stages, and each one has to be earned before the next one starts.

items: Rebuild basic loading capacity in the ranges that don't provoke anything
Reintroduce the sport's specific movement patterns, at low intensity, on purpose
Push real strength back into those same patterns
Add power and reactive work once strength holds under fatigue
Return to full sport-speed, at full intensity, last — not first

blocks.text: The rule

blocks.label: Rule
blocks.text: An injury doesn't end when the pain does. It ends when every stage above has actually been earned — not skipped because the pain went quiet early. Rush the order and the pain has a way of coming back to collect what it's owed.
